annotations.label: Difference engine

anchor.snippet: A difference engine is an automatic mechanical calculator designed to tabulate polynomial functions

annotations.note: A physical mechanical calculator; Mathlib formalizes mathematics, not hardware devices.

annotations.label: Method of finite differences

anchor.snippet: a way to interpolate or tabulate functions by using a small set of polynomial co-efficients

annotations.note: The forward-difference operator `fwdDiff` and the Gregory-Newton formula `shift_eq_sum_fwdDiff_iter` formalize tabulating/interpolating a function from its finite differences.

annotations.label: Column addition rule

anchor.snippet: can only add the value of a column n + 1 to column n to produce the new value of n

annotations.note: This is a mechanical update rule of the machine's columns, not a mathematical statement in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Programming via initial values

anchor.snippet: The engine is programmed by setting initial values to the columns

annotations.note: An operational description of programming the machine, with no corresponding Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Iteration per shaft rotation

anchor.snippet: one iteration (i.e. one full set of addition and carry operations) happens for each rotation of the main shaft

annotations.note: A mechanical timing fact of the engine, not a mathematical statement.

annotations.label: Four-step iteration cycle

anchor.snippet: Each iteration creates a new result, and is accomplished in four steps corresponding to four complete turns of the handle

annotations.note: A description of the machine's operating cycle, not formalizable mathematics.

annotations.label: Subtraction via ten's complement

anchor.snippet: The engine represents negative numbers as ten's complements

annotations.note: Ten's-complement number representation is not formalized as such in Mathlib (which has modular `ZMod` arithmetic but no ten's-complement encoding).

annotations.label: Principle is method of divided differences

anchor.snippet: The principle of a difference engine is Newton's method of divided differences

annotations.note: Mathlib has the forward (finite) difference operator `fwdDiff` actually used by the engine, but Newton's divided-difference scheme itself is not formalized.

annotations.label: Tabulating a quadratic polynomial

anchor.snippet: with the goal of tabulating the values

annotations.note: The worked quadratic example is an instance of the general Gregory-Newton tabulation formula `shift_eq_sum_fwdDiff_iter`, but no such concrete example is a Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Constant (n+1)th difference of degree-n polynomial

anchor.snippet: by starting with any polynomial of degree n , the column number n + 1 will always be constant

annotations.note: `Polynomial.fwdDiff_iter_degree_eq_factorial` proves the n-th forward difference of a degree-n polynomial is the constant `leadingCoeff • n!` (with `fwdDiff_iter_degree_add_one_eq_zero` giving that the next difference vanishes).

annotations.label: Storage to tabulate degree-n polynomials

anchor.snippet: To tabulate polynomials of degree n , one needs sufficient storage to hold n numbers

annotations.note: A statement about the machine's storage requirement, not a mathematical theorem in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Initial values by backtracking

anchor.snippet: The initial values of columns can be calculated by first manually calculating N consecutive values of the function and by backtracking

annotations.note: `fwdDiff_iter_eq_sum_shift` expresses the n-th forward difference as a combination of consecutive function values, the mathematical content of backtracking, but the backtracking procedure itself is not a Mathlib decl.

annotations.label: Initial values from polynomial coefficients

anchor.snippet: the initial values can be calculated directly from the constant coefficients

annotations.note: Mathlib relates iterated forward differences to polynomial coefficients (e.g. `Polynomial.fwdDiff_iter_degree_eq_factorial`), but the direct closed-form for all initial column values is not stated as one declaration.

annotations.label: Analytic functions as power series

anchor.snippet: Many commonly used functions are analytic functions , which can be expressed as power series

annotations.note: `AnalyticAt` (via `HasFPowerSeriesAt`/`HasFPowerSeriesOnBall`) defines a function as analytic exactly when it has a convergent power-series expansion.

annotations.label: Taylor series from derivatives

anchor.snippet: The Taylor series expresses the function as a sum obtained from its derivatives at one point

annotations.note: `taylorWithin`/`taylorCoeffWithin` define the Taylor polynomial as a sum of iterated-derivative terms, and `Complex.hasSum_taylorSeries_on_ball` shows the series sums to the function.

annotations.label: Maclaurin series at zero

anchor.snippet: Setting 0 as the start of computation we get the simplified Maclaurin series

annotations.note: The Maclaurin series is the Taylor series `taylorWithin` at base point 0, but Mathlib has no dedicated Maclaurin declaration.

annotations.label: Curve fitting bounds maximum error

anchor.snippet: A solution which guarantees a constant maximum error is to use curve fitting

annotations.note: Mathlib has Chebyshev extremal properties underlying minimax approximation, but no curve-fitting/equioscillation theorem guaranteeing a constant maximum error.

annotations.label: Polynomial interpolation via Gaussian reduction

anchor.snippet: an N −1th degree polynomial interpolation of the function is found

annotations.note: `Lagrange.interpolate` (with `eq_interpolate`/`degree_interpolate_lt`) gives the unique interpolating polynomial of degree < N through N nodes, the same result though constructed via Lagrange rather than Gaussian elimination.
